title: Miss Julie
paragraphs: PREFACE (excerpt): Miss Julie is a naturalistic tragedy. The time is Midsummer Eve; the place, a large kitchen in the country house of a Count. The characters are: Miss Julie, the Count's daughter, twenty-five years old; Jean, a valet, thirty years old; and Christine, a cook, thirty-five years old.

SCENE: A large kitchen. The ceiling and walls are partly hidden by draperies and greenery. The rear wall runs diagonally from the left and there is a speaking tube on it. At the right there is a large arch through which one sees an avenue of trees. On the table are some lilacs. Christine is standing at the stove frying something. She wears a light-colored cotton dress and an apron.
JEAN: Miss Julie's crazy again to-night—absolutely crazy!
CHRISTINE: Oh, you're here at last!

JEAN: I went with the Count to the station, and when I came back, I passed the barn and went in to dance. And there was Miss Julie leading the dance with the gamekeeper. But when she set eyes on me, she rushed right up to me and asked me to waltz with her. And the way she waltzed—I've never seen anything like it. She's crazy!
CHRISTINE: She's always been crazy, but never so bad as these last two weeks, since the engagement was broken off.

JEAN: Well, what was the matter then? He was a good enough fellow, wasn't he? Though he wasn't rich, of course. Oh, but that's just it! He refused to lower himself. (He sits down.) But isn't it queer that a young lady—hm!—should prefer to stay home here rather than follow her father to relatives?
CHRISTINE: Oh, I suppose she doesn't want to see him embarrassed—seeing that she broke the engagement herself.
JEAN: No doubt! But what was the story? He was a good-looking fellow. (He pours wine into a glass and puts the glass to his lips.)
CHRISTINE: Don't do that! Don't you know the Count will see how much is gone?
JEAN: Go on! Of course he'll notice. He notices everything. (He puts back the glass.) But never mind—he's not home to-night. (He sits down again.)
CHRISTINE: And his daughter?
JEAN: Miss Julie? (He stands up.) She doesn't care. She gives me the eye herself.
CHRISTINE: Oh, she does!
JEAN: She's been hanging around here all evening. She's alone, too; nobody to keep her company.
(MISS JULIE appears in the upper entrance.)
MISS JULIE: Are you coming?
JEAN: Yes, at once, Miss Julie.
(She goes out again. JEAN remains.)
CHRISTINE: He's going with her now—and I suppose I may be sure she'll have him home well before morning. He goes with her, as a real companion should—since the Count is away.
JEAN: Don't be stupid! (He takes some food from the table and puts it in his mouth, tasting it.) Everything's going to be all right.
(Exit.)
(MISS JULIE comes in again, followed by JEAN.)
MISS JULIE: (to JEAN) Don't go yet! Stay and talk with me.
JEAN: Will you not be careful, Miss Julie? No one is to know about this.
MISS JULIE: (with sudden boldness) What have I got to be afraid of?
JEAN: (in a lower tone) What if the Count should come home unexpectedly?
MISS JULIE: My father! Good heavens, no! He's not at home, and he won't be back till after midnight. You know that as well as I do. What harm is there in talking?
JEAN: And what would people say?
MISS JULIE: What would they say? We are dancing! It's Midsummer Night!
JEAN: (gravely) Miss Julie, you should go to your room. I am only a servant. Please understand.
MISS JULIE: (sitting down) Oh, don't be tiresome. Sit down here beside me. Tell me something.
JEAN: I don't know what to tell you.
MISS JULIE: (leaning back) Don't you know any stories?
JEAN: Oh, plenty of stories. But I can't tell them now—not here.
MISS JULIE: Then we'll talk about you. Tell me about yourself.
JEAN: (sitting reluctantly) There's not much to tell. I'm only a servant. My life was hard when I was young.
MISS JULIE: Tell me from the beginning.

JEAN: (after a pause) Well—when I was a boy, we were very poor. I remember going hungry. My mother worked in the fields. I used to play around this estate as a child, long before I became a servant here. Once I hid in the garden and saw you, Miss Julie, playing on the lawn. You were a little girl in a white dress. And I thought—you were so beautiful—like an angel. I could never speak to you. You were too far above me.
MISS JULIE: (with interest) And now?
JEAN: Now I am your father's valet. Life changes.
MISS JULIE: Do you still think I'm an angel?
JEAN: I don't know what I think. I think that you are—dangerous.
MISS JULIE: (laughing) Dangerous! That's a new idea.
JEAN: (seriously) You are, Miss Julie. You play with fire. You don't know what you're doing.
MISS JULIE: And you know?
JEAN: Better than you, I think.
(A long pause. MISS JULIE is pensive.)
MISS JULIE: (suddenly) Let's go out. I want to dance again.
JEAN: With me?
MISS JULIE: Who else?
JEAN: It will be talked about.
MISS JULIE: I don't care. Let them talk.
(She takes his arm and they go out. The stage is empty for a moment. Then CHRISTINE comes back in, clears the table, and begins to prepare for bed.)
CHRISTINE: (to herself) Something is wrong tonight. Something is wrong with Miss Julie. She's been strange for weeks—ever since the Count's engagement was broken. God knows where it will end.
(She puts out the light over the stove and goes to her room. The kitchen is lit only by the bright summer night coming through the windows. MISS JULIE and JEAN return.)
JEAN: You see? It's late. They've all gone to bed.
MISS JULIE: (flushed, excited) I don't care. I'm not tired.
JEAN: (seriously) Miss Julie—you should go in. This is—it's not right.
MISS JULIE: (stopping) What's the matter? Are you afraid?
JEAN: Not for myself.
MISS JULIE: (moving closer) Then for me?
JEAN: (stepping back) Yes. For you, Miss Julie. You don't understand what you're doing.
MISS JULIE: I understand perfectly. I am the Count's daughter, and you are my father's valet, and tonight is Midsummer Night, and I am tired of being proper and respectable, and I want—
JEAN: What do you want?
MISS JULIE: (after a long pause, quietly) I don't know. I want to fall. I want to—cease being what I am. Just for one night.
(A long silence.)
JEAN: That is not something that can be undone.
MISS JULIE: I know.
JEAN: Miss Julie—once this thing happens, you can never go back. You understand that?
MISS JULIE: Yes. I understand.
(A pause. Then JEAN moves toward her. Suddenly KRISTIN'S voice is heard off-stage, and they separate.)
JEAN: (hastily) They're up. We must be careful.
(He goes out. MISS JULIE remains, her face white. Then she straightens and sits down at the table. JEAN returns.)
JEAN: Kristin is awake. We must be very careful.
MISS JULIE: (quietly) What are we going to do?
JEAN: We?
MISS JULIE: Yes. We. I can't undo what has happened. Neither can you.
JEAN: (after a pause) No. We can't undo it.
MISS JULIE: What do people do—in situations like this?
JEAN: People of my class run away. People of your class—I don't know.
MISS JULIE: (standing) I will run away too. Will you come with me?
JEAN: And go where?
MISS JULIE: Switzerland. Italy. Anywhere. We can open a hotel—you know about hotels, you told me. We can start a new life.
JEAN: (with reluctant excitement) It's possible. If we had money.
MISS JULIE: I have money. I can get money.
JEAN: How much?
MISS JULIE: I don't know exactly. But enough to start.
(They begin to plan in low, urgent voices, as the summer light outside grows slowly brighter—the short northern night already ending.)
JEAN: (suddenly stopping) Listen. The Count's bell.
(Both freeze.)
MISS JULIE: (whispering) He's not supposed to be back until—
JEAN: The Count's bell. I must go.
(He starts to move. MISS JULIE catches his arm.)
MISS JULIE: No. Wait. Jean—
JEAN: (his face changed—harder, more distant now) I must answer the Count's bell, Miss Julie. That is my duty.
MISS JULIE: (releasing him) Yes. Go.
(JEAN goes. MISS JULIE stands alone in the kitchen. The morning light comes in through the windows. KRISTIN enters, dressed for church.)
KRISTIN: Good morning, Miss Julie. You're up early.
MISS JULIE: (quietly) Yes, Kristin. I haven't slept.
KRISTIN: (looking at her) Are you all right, Miss Julie?
MISS JULIE: (with an odd laugh) All right? Yes, Kristin. I am perfectly all right.
(KRISTIN looks at her a moment, then goes about her morning work. JEAN enters again, in his livery.)
JEAN: (in a formal, distant tone) The Count asks if Miss Julie would please come up.
MISS JULIE: (looking at him—at this stranger in livery) The Count asks.
JEAN: (meeting her eyes) Yes, Miss Julie.
(A long pause. Then MISS JULIE picks up the razor that is lying on the table—JEAN has been sharpening his master's razors. She holds it for a moment, looking at it.)
JEAN: (quietly, in his own voice again) Miss Julie.
MISS JULIE: Tell me what to do.
JEAN: (very quietly) Go.
MISS JULIE: (to herself, as if in a trance) Yes. Go. It is the only thing.
(She walks slowly toward the door. She pauses and looks back once at JEAN. Then she goes out. JEAN stands alone.)
KRISTIN: (looking after her) What was that all about?
JEAN: (after a long silence) I don't know. (A pause.) God only knows.
(He stands very still. Outside, the summer morning blazes with light. A razor lies on the table.)
CURTAIN.
